Project Guidance Report

DRAM Media Server Ultimate v5.0

Initial Project Analysis

The DRAM Media Server Ultimate v5.0 is a self-hosted media server designed for personal use. It scans local video files, generates thumbnails, streams content via a web interface, and offers remote control for the MPV media player. The project emphasizes a robust, modern, and user-friendly experience accessible across devices, functioning as a Progressive Web App (PWA).

Key Features:

  • Smart Scanner: Multi-threaded, filters system junk, prevents loops, auto-generates thumbnails.
  • MPV Remote Control: Manages a playback queue, supports auto-next, and allows control from a phone.
  • Modular Frontend: PWA compatibility and a responsive interface (desktop sidebar, mobile bottom navigation).
  • Stable Mechanism: Uses SQLite asynchronously for reliability and auto-detects the operating environment.

The project is structured with a Node.js backend (Express.js, Socket.IO, SQLite) and a frontend built with vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. It leverages fluent-ffmpeg for media processing and child_process for MPV interaction. The database schema includes tables for videos, playback history, thumbnails, and settings.

Generate Tech Stack Recommendations

Layer/Component Option 1 (Recommended) Option 2 (Alternative) Strategic Alignment
Backend Framework Node.js (Express.js) Node.js (Fastify) Lightweight, performant, mature ecosystem for REST APIs and real-time communication. Fastify offers slightly better performance.
Database SQLite (with sqlite/sqlite3) IndexedDB (for PWA-only local cache) Server-side file-based database for simplicity and cross-platform compatibility. High performance for a single user.
Real-time Communication Socket.IO WebSockets API (native) Provides robust real-time bidirectional communication, ideal for scanner progress and MPV status updates. Socket.IO adds useful abstractions.
Frontend Framework Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS (PWA) Vue.js (for reactive components) Focus on lightweight, fast loading, and native browser features for PWA. Vue.js could simplify complex UI interactions.
Media Processing fluent-ffmpeg ffmpeg-static + child_process Robust library for interacting with FFmpeg, simplifying thumbnail generation and video metadata extraction.
CSS Framework Tailwind CSS SCSS/PostCSS Utility-first approach for rapid and consistent styling, highly customizable. SCSS offers more traditional CSS pre-processing.
Process Management Node.js worker_threads Node.js child_process (fork) Efficiently offloads CPU-intensive tasks (scanning, thumbnailing) without blocking the main event loop.

Research And Propose Optimal Solutions And Libraries

The current selection of libraries and solutions aligns well with the project's goals of a lightweight, performant, and self-hosted media server for a single user.

  • SQLite: Excellent choice for embedded, zero-configuration databases, perfect for a personal server. The use of WAL mode and NORMAL synchronous mode are good optimizations for balancing performance and data integrity.
  • Node.js worker_threads: A critical decision for the scanner, ensuring the UI remains responsive during long-running I/O and CPU-bound tasks.
  • MPV for Playback: Leveraging an external, highly capable media player like MPV via IPC is a smart move, avoiding the complexities of building an entire media player within Node.js.
  • PWA Frontend: Provides a native app-like experience without app store distribution, enhancing accessibility and user engagement.

Generate Code Improvements And Fixes

Identified Bugs

1. PWA Application Icon (Critical)

Description: client/public/manifest.json specifies a 1x1 transparent PNG as the sole icon, leading to an invisible or broken icon on home screens, negatively impacting UX and branding.

Location: client/public/manifest.json

Suggested Fix: Replace the placeholder data:image/png;base64,... with references to actual icon files in various sizes (e.g., 192x192, 512x512 pixels).

2. Silent ALTER TABLE Error Suppression in schema.js (Medium)

Description: The ensure helper function in verifyAndMigrate uses a broad try...catch (e) {} for ALTER TABLE commands. While intended to ignore "column already exists" errors, it suppresses *any* SQL error during schema modification, hindering debugging of configuration or code mistakes.

Location: server/v2/core/schema.js, ensure function.

Suggested Fix: Refine the catch block to specifically check for "column already exists" error codes/messages or log other errors with a warning.

3. FFmpeg ffprobe and screenshots Timeout Handling (Medium)

Description: Fixed timeouts (5s for ffprobe, 8s for screenshots) are applied. If FFmpeg takes longer for large, corrupted, or slow files, these operations silently resolve with default values (0 duration) or false (failed thumbnail), potentially causing incomplete data or missing thumbnails without clear error indication.

Location: server/v2/core/scanner.worker.js, processVideo function.

Suggested Fix:

  • Increase timeouts or make them configurable for large files.
  • Log warnings when timeouts occur, specifying affected files.
  • Implement more robust FFmpeg error handling by analyzing its error output.

4. MPV IPC Connection Heuristic Delays (Medium)

Description:

  • server/v2/services/mpv.service.js uses a fixed 1-second setTimeout to wait for MPV's IPC server, prone to race conditions.
  • server/v2/controllers/mpv.controller.js uses a 500ms setTimeout in the play function after mpvService.stop() to ensure MPV termination and resource release, also relying on an unreliable fixed delay.

Location: server/v2/services/mpv.service.js (startMpv), server/v2/controllers/mpv.controller.js (play).

Suggested Fix:

  • For IPC connection: Implement a retry-with-backoff mechanism or poll for IPC socket/pipe availability.
  • For MPV restart: Wait for the close event of the child process or implement more sophisticated IPC pipe management.

Performance & Optimization Opportunities

1. Frontend API Error Handling Consistency

Opportunity: Current lenient error handling (e.g., getPlaybackPos returning { position: 0 } on error) might mask server/network issues.

Recommendation: Implement more granular error handling in client/public/js/modules/api.js to distinguish error types and provide informative feedback.

2. Service Worker Caching for PWA

Opportunity: The current service-worker.js is minimal and lacks caching strategies, leading to re-downloading assets on each visit and preventing offline functionality.

Recommendation: Enhance service-worker.js to precache static assets and implement a cache-first or stale-while-revalidate strategy for runtime requests.

3. fs.statSync in streamVideo

Opportunity: Using synchronous fs.statSync in server/v2/controllers/video.controller.js (streamVideo) can block the event loop, especially under concurrent load.

Recommendation: Replace fs.statSync with the asynchronous await fs.promises.stat.

Best Practices & Style Recommendations

1. CORS Wildcard Origin (*) (Security/Best Practice Violation)

Issue: Socket.IO (cors: { origin: "*" }) and Express (app.use(cors())) use a wildcard * for CORS, which is a security risk in production environments by allowing any domain to make requests.

Location: server/v2/index.js

Recommendation: Restrict CORS to specific origins (e.g., http://localhost:3001 or the server's local IP).

2. Environment Variable Management (Best Practice)

Issue: Configuration values (port, dbPath, defaultScanPaths) are hardcoded or derived directly, requiring code changes for customization.

Recommendation: Adopt a library like dotenv to manage configuration via a .env file, separating environment-specific settings from the codebase.

3. Global window.app Instance (Style/Maintainability)

Issue: Exposing the application instance globally via window.app = this; in client/public/js/main.js pollutes the global namespace and complicates dependency management.

Recommendation: Remove window.app = this; and attach event listeners directly using DOM methods (document.getElementById().addEventListener()).

4. JSDoc Comments (General Improvement)

Issue: Code comments are sparse, impacting maintainability and understanding for future modifications or contributions.

Recommendation: Add JSDoc comments to functions, classes, and complex logic blocks to detail purpose, parameters, return values, and side effects.

5. Input Validation on API Endpoints (Best Practice/Security)

Issue: Endpoints like /api/videos/:id/playback, /api/mpv/play, etc., directly use req.params.id or req.body.id/action without explicit validation.

Recommendation: Implement input validation (e.g., using express-validator or manual checks) to ensure parameters are of the expected type and format, adding robustness against malformed requests.

Generate Refined Project Code Output

This section would contain the full, refined project code. For demonstration purposes, a small illustrative snippet is provided, showcasing a potential improvement.

// server/v2/core/schema.js - Refined ensure function for specific error handling
async function ensure(db, tableName, columnName, columnDefinition) {
    try {
        await db.run(`ALTER TABLE ${tableName} ADD COLUMN ${columnName} ${columnDefinition}`);
        // console.log(`Column '${columnName}' added to table '${tableName}'.`);
    } catch (e) {
        // Specifically check for 'duplicate column' error (SQLite error code 1, detail 'duplicate column name')
        if (e.message.includes('duplicate column name')) {
            // console.log(`Column '${columnName}' already exists in table '${tableName}'. Skipping.`);
        } else {
            console.error(`Error adding column '${columnName}' to table '${tableName}':`, e.message);
            throw e; // Re-throw other unexpected errors
        }
    }
}

// server/v2/controllers/video.controller.js - Example for fs.promises.stat
import fs from 'node:fs/promises'; // For async file operations

const streamVideo = async (req, res) => {
    const videoPath = req.params.path; // Assume path is resolved and validated upstream
    try {
        const stat = await fs.stat(videoPath);
        const fileSize = stat.size;
        // ... rest of the streaming logic ...
    } catch (error) {
        console.error('Error streaming video:', error);
        res.status(500).send('Error streaming video');
    }
};

Generate Technical Documentation

The DRAM Media Server Ultimate v5.0 project has been meticulously designed with a clear architectural separation between its backend services and frontend PWA. This documentation outlines the core components and their interactions.

1. Backend Core

  • EnvironmentV2 (server/v2/config/env.js): A robust class for detecting the operating platform (Termux, WSL, Windows, Linux) and dynamically configuring paths and ports. This ensures high portability and adaptability across different deployment scenarios.
  • Database Module (server/v2/core/database.js): Establishes a singleton SQLite database connection, applying performance-enhancing pragmas like WAL and NORMAL synchronous mode for improved concurrency and data safety.
  • Schema Manager (server/v2/core/schema.js): Automates database schema creation and migration on application startup, ensuring that tables and necessary columns are always up-to-date.
  • Server Entry Point (server/v2/index.js): Orchestrates the Express app, HTTP server, and Socket.IO. Handles middleware, static file serving, and initializes core services and API routes.

2. Logic & API Services

  • Scanner Worker (server/v2/core/scanner.worker.js): A multi-threaded worker responsible for intelligent video scanning and thumbnail generation, filtering irrelevant directories and skipping existing thumbnails for efficiency.
  • Scanner Service (server/v2/services/scanner.service.js): Manages the scanner worker thread, communicates progress via Socket.IO, and updates the database with video metadata.
  • Video Controller (server/v2/controllers/video.controller.js): Handles API requests for video listing, playback history management (INSERT OR REPLACE for updates), and efficient byte-range video streaming.
  • MPV Service (server/v2/services/mpv.service.js): Manages the MPV player process, facilitates IPC communication via named pipes/Unix sockets, and controls the playback queue with auto-next functionality.

3. Frontend (PWA)

  • API Gateway (client/public/js/modules/api.js): Centralized module for all backend API interactions.
  • UI Renderer (client/public/js/modules/ui.js): Responsible for dynamically rendering video grids, updating scan statuses, and managing UI selection visuals.
  • VideoPlayer (client/public/js/modules/player.js): Manages the in-browser video player overlay, including playback controls, transformations (rotate, zoom), and auto-saving playback positions.
  • DataManager (client/public/js/modules/manager.js): Handles application data state, filtering, sorting, video selection, and playlist export functionality.
  • Main Application Logic (client/public/js/main.js): Orchestrates the entire frontend, sets up Socket.IO for real-time updates, manages global event listeners, and integrates MPV remote control.

Generate Minimal Deployment Guide

This guide provides a quick setup for deploying and running the DRAM Media Server Ultimate v5.0.

🚀 HOÀN TẤT & HƯỚNG DẪN KHỞI ĐỘNG CUỐI CÙNG

1. Install Dependencies:

npm install

This command will install all necessary Node.js packages as defined in package.json.

2. Start Server:

npm run dev:v2

This script will launch the Node.js backend server, which also serves the frontend PWA.

3. Experience:

  • Access: Open http://[Server-IP]:3001 on a phone or other device on the same network.
  • PWA: For a native-like app experience, use your browser's "Add to Home Screen" feature.
  • Functionality:
    • Click "Quét" (Scan) to initiate video scanning and load content.
    • Click "Remote" to control the MPV player running on your PC.

Conclusion

The DRAM Media Server Ultimate v5.0 is a robust and well-conceived project that successfully meets its core feature goals. The use of Node.js features like worker_threads and database ON CONFLICT clauses demonstrates a good understanding of efficient and reliable backend development.

Prioritized Next Steps:

  1. Critical PWA Icon Bug: Immediately replace the placeholder icon in manifest.json with proper image assets.
  2. MPV IPC Reliability: Enhance MPV IPC by replacing heuristic setTimeout delays with more robust polling or event-driven mechanisms.
  3. Service Worker Caching: Implement caching in service-worker.js for improved PWA performance and offline functionality.
  4. CORS Configuration: Restrict CORS from wildcard (*) to specific origins for security best practices.
  5. Environment Variable Management: Adopt dotenv for flexible and maintainable configuration.

Addressing these points will significantly improve the system's stability, user experience, and adherence to software development best practices.
